coredata.dc:title: Long carbon chain-modified carbon nanoparticles for oil displacement in harsh-condition reservoirs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Wei
Sun, Yue
Li, Lin
Wang, Xiaolong
Zhao, Mingwei
Dai, Caili
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles, as an emerging type of enhanced oil recovery agent, promising broad prospects in unconventional oil and gas development. However, limitations such as low interfacial activity due to the strong hydrophilicity of carbon nanoparticles and tendency to aggregate under high-temperature and high-salinity conditions restrict their further application. In this study, activity carbon dots (S-CDs) were prepared via a simple two-step hydrothermal reaction. A range of physicochemical techniques characterized S-CDs, revealing their small particle size (1.4 ± 0.01 nm) and narrow distribution, maintaining strong dispersion stability in mineralized water at 130 °C with a concentration of 16 × 104 mg/L. The AFM scanning results indicated that S-CDs possessed strong interfacial wetting control capability, the adhesion force between oil and rock cores (after S-CDs treatment) decreased by 25 times. Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) confirmed that S-CD adsorbed at the oil-water interface could formed a solid film, which could rupture into smaller oil droplets for convenient backward migration. The results demonstrated that S-CDs improved the interfacial activity while maintaining the hydrophilicity of CDs. In core displacement experiments, 0.05 wt% S-CDs nanofluid reduced injection pressure by 32.4 % and increased recovery rates by 34.9 %. This work provides new insights for the development of high-temperature, high-salinity displacement agents in harsh-condition reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Interface properties regulation
Oil film stripping
Enhanced oil recovery
Pressure reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image (a), corresponding statistics of particle distribution (b), AFM 2D (c), and 3D images (d) of S-CDs. The width and height profiles along the line in the 2D image (e).
FT-IR spectra of S-CDs (a), survey scan XPS spectrum in the binding energy range 0–1400 eV (b) and high-resolution spectra of O1s (c), N1s (d), C1s (e) of S-CDs.
Average diameter (a) and zeta potential (b) of S-CDs before and after aging under varying salinity.
Average diameter (a) and zeta potential (b) of S-CDs before and after aging exposure to varying temperatures.
Absorbance curve with time (a) and interfacial tension curves (b) of S-CDs.
The interface modulus (a) and phase angle (b) change with time.

Diagrams (inset) and frequency histograms of adhesion force between alkane and core: SFW treated core (a), H-CDs treated core (b), Z-Si treated core (c), S-CDs (d) treated core. The contact angles of oil (e) in SFW, H-CDs, Z-Si and S-CDs.
The change of emulsification degree (a) and water separation rate curve (b).
Schematic diagram of oil film peeling (a), variation of residual oil proportion curve (b), mechanism diagram (c), and adhesion diagram (inset) and frequency histogram between crude oils (d).
Velocity distribution around oil droplets after adsorption of different nanoparticles: (a) SFW, (b) H-CDs, (c) Z-Si, (d) S-CDs and (e) corresponding rate distribution curve graph.
The injecting pressure (a) and oil recovery (b) during SFW, H-CDs, Z-Si and S-CDs nanofluid flooding.
Enhanced oil recovery and injection pressure reduction mechanism diagram of S-CDs (a), LCSM images of core sections before (b) and after (c) S-CDs nanofluid injection.
The synthetic route for the S-CDs.
Results of oil displacement performance of different nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Long carbon chain-modified carbon nanoparticles for oil displacement in harsh-condition reservoirs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development (China University of Petroleum (East China)), Ministry of Education, Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development (China University of Petroleum (East China))

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Branch of CNOOC Limited, Tianjin 300459, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfield Chemistry, Department of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfield Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles, as an emerging type of enhanced oil recovery agent, promising broad prospects in unconventional oil and gas development. However, limitations such as low interfacial activity due to the strong hydrophilicity of carbon nanoparticles and tendency to aggregate under high-temperature and high-salinity conditions restrict their further application. In this study, activity carbon dots (S-CDs) were prepared via a simple two-step hydrothermal reaction. A range of physicochemical techniques characterized S-CDs, revealing their small particle size (1.4 ± 0.01 nm) and narrow distribution, maintaining strong dispersion stability in mineralized water at 130 °C with a concentration of 16 × 104 mg/L. The AFM scanning results indicated that S-CDs possessed strong interfacial wetting control capability, the adhesion force between oil and rock cores (after S-CDs treatment) decreased by 25 times. Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) confirmed that S-CD adsorbed at the oil-water interface could formed a solid film, which could rupture into smaller oil droplets for convenient backward migration. The results demonstrated that S-CDs improved the interfacial activity while maintaining the hydrophilicity of CDs. In core displacement experiments, 0.05 wt% S-CDs nanofluid reduced injection pressure by 32.4 % and increased recovery rates by 34.9 %. This work provides new insights for the development of high-temperature, high-salinity displacement agents in harsh-condition reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The efficient development of crude oil resources is of paramount importance in ensuring human energy security and stability [1]. Of these, unconventional oil makes up 46.24 % of the world's oil reserves, and this percentage is still rising [2]. Unconventional petroleum reserves, such as tight oil and shale oil, present unique challenges in the extraction process due to their characteristics of low porosity, low permeability, and heterogeneity [3–5]. The most current mainstream method for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) is water flooding. However, because of the high viscosity of crude oil and the high fluidity of the displacement agents (water), viscous fingering occurs, which reduces oil production [6–9]. In this sense, chemical flooding agent represented by surfactants and polymers can effectively resolve such issues [10–13]. Surfactants can contribute to enhanced water-wettability and reduced interfacial tension [14,15], while polymers can greatly increase solution viscosity [16–18]. However, the use of polymers and surfactants is restricted due to their high cost, sensitivity to extreme environmental factors (such as high salinity and temperature), and adsorption issues dependent on the characteristics of reservoir rocks [19,20]. Recent studies have shown that nanoparticles possess specific physical and chemical properties and application potential in enhanced oil recovery, which can provide a new perspective for the development of unconventional reservoirs [15,21–25].

Carbon nanoparticles exhibited superior properties and greater potential in harsh-condition reservoirs, with ultra-small size facilitating the entry of micro and nano-scale pore throats, abundant functional groups facilitating the further modification, and intrinsic hydrophilicity endowing excellent dispersion and salt tolerance [26–29]. Although the large number of hydrophilic functional groups on the carbon nanoparticle surface endow high dispersion stability, they also restrict the activity of nanoparticles at the oil–water interface, hindering the detachment of crude oil from the core [30–32]. Thus, researchers have employed various modification methods, such as introducing hydrophobic chemical groups [33] or grafting surfactants [34] for chemical modification, to enhance affinity for organic compounds, ultimately achieving hydrophilic lipophilic balance (HLB) [35–37]. However, traditional modification methods can only elevate the thermal and salt resistance of carbon nanoparticles to 100 °C and 14 × 104 mg/L, respectively, which is far from sufficient for the development of harsh-condition reservoirs [36]. The simultaneous enhancement of both temperature and salt resistance, and oil–water interfacial activity, remains a challenge [38,39].

In this paper, S-CDs with abundant surface functional groups were prepared via a simple two-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Long hydrophobic chains containing specific functional groups were introduced onto the surface of carbon nanoparticles. Through the synergistic effect between the intrinsic hydrophilic groups and the grafted hydrophobic long chains, S-CDs demonstrated excellent temperature and salt resistance as well as high oil–water interfacial activity. Utilizing carbon nanoparticles (H-CDs) and silicon nanoparticles (Z-Si) [40] comparison, this paper explored aspects such as oil–water interface properties (emulsification efficiency, interfacial modulus), oil-solid interface control, and microscopic oil displacement mechanisms. This study can pave the way for the practical application of carbon-based nanomaterials in harsh oil reservoirs.

The chemicals citric acid monohydrate (CA, C6H8O7, 99 %), ethanediamine (EDA, C2H8N2, 99 %), absolute ethanol (C2H6O, 99 %), hydrochloric acid (AR, HCl, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %) and inorganic salts were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cocamidopropyl betaine (CAB-35, 99 %) was sourced from Shanghai Chuxing Chemical Co., Ltd. The dialysis bags (MD44, 1000 molecular weight) were obtained from Zhongke Yikang (Beijing) Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Cores with a permeability of about 0.5 mD were obtained from Hai’an Petroleum Scientific Research Instrument Co., Ltd. The crude oil used in the experiment was acquired in the Ta Yilian oilfield.

1.3 g of CA and 0.5 g of EDA were dissolved in deionized water (50 mL) and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined hydrothermal reactor. Then, the sealed autoclave was kept at 200 °C for 12 h to obtain CDs. 0.34 g of CAB-35 was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol, and then 0.05 g of dilute hydrochloric acid was added dropwise with stirring. After stirring continuously for 2 h, the ethanol solution was evaporated to yield HCAB. The obtained HCAB was dispersed in CDs and reacted in a PTFE-lined 100 mL autoclave at 90 °C for 3 h, followed by adding NaOH aqueous solution dropwise until pH = 7 (Measurements were made using pH test strips) to obtain an aqueous solution with activated carbon dots (S-CDs). A 0.1 μm filter and a 1000 D dialysis bag were used to filter the resultant solution until no color remained, and the product was then freeze-dried to obtain pure S-CDs. Scheme 1 showed the systematic synthetic route for the S-CDs.

The obtained samples were examined by a series of physicochemical techniques to explore the chemical and structural properties. The morphology was observed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan). The size distribution and adhesion force of the S-CDs were measured using an atomic force microscope (AFM, MultiMode8 SPM, Germany). FT-IR spectra of the S-CDs were recorded using a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer (America) in the frequency range of 4000–500 cm−1 using the KBr pellet pressing method. XPS surface analysis was performed using an XPS microscope (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi, America) with Kα radiation source (1486.6 eV). Each binding energy was calibrated using the C1s peak at 284.8 eV. The contact angle was determined using a contact angle measurement instrument (JC2000D2, China). Interfacial tension and Interface modulus were measured using the interfacial rheometer (TECLIS Scientific Racker-S, France). The Zeta potential and hydrated particle size of the S-CDs were analyzed using a dynamic light scattering particle size analyzer and Zeta potential analyzer (NanoBrook Omni, Brookhaven Instruments, America). Laser confocal scanning microscopy (LCSM, KEYENCE, VK-X250) was used for 3D imaging and the roughness measurement.

Diffusion double layer of active carbon points would be compressed and dispersion stability would be lowered in formation water with a high salinity due to an abundance of cations. High temperature could also intensify the Brownian motion of charged ions, which raised the likelihood of collisions and made them easier to clump together. Therefore, temperature and salt resistance were crucial factors for assessing the effectiveness of oil displacement agents. The hydration size and Zeta potential of S-CDs were examined to assess their temperature resistance and salt tolerance properties before and after aging.

The oil–water interfacial tension (IFT) and elastic modulus of S-CDs nanofluids with varying crude oil concentrations (0.001–0.5 wt%) were measured at 90 °C. At a frequency of 0.1 Hz and an amplitude area of 10 %, the interface rheological characteristics were measured. After treating a quartz glass sheet with asphaltene derived from crude oil at 130 °C for 7 d, the glass sheet was immersed in simulated formation water (SFW), H-CDs, Z-Si and S-CDs nanofluids for 24 h. Subsequently, the underwater oil contact angle of the glass sheet was measured. An ultraviolet spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of S-CDs nanofluids at multiple concentrations using the following equation:
Q =
(
C 0
-
C X
)
V 1
M S

In this equation, C0 represented the initial concentration (mg/L), Cx represented the concentration after adsorption (mg/L), Vl was the volume of the solution (L), ms was the mass of the sand particles (g), and Q denoted the adsorption capacity (mg/g) [41].

Scheme 2 illustrated the evaluation method of the oil displacement performance of S-CDs nanofluids. Oil was injected into the core under vacuum until it was saturated and then aged at 130 °C for 7 d to simulate oil distribution. Experimental parameters included a test temperature of 130 °C, an injection flow rate of 0.1 mL/min, a confining pressure difference of 3 MPa, and a 1 MPa back pressure. The saturated core was filled with SFW, when oil production stopped and the injection pressure was stable, a certain volume of S-CDs nanofluid was injected after the pressure was released. Pressure was once more released for water injection after standing for 12 h, real-time records were made of the injection pressure and displacement oil volume simultaneously. Table 1 displayed core physical parameters (length, porosity, oil saturation and permeability, reservoir properties, core type, and surface properties).

HR-TEM and AFM were employed to observe the morphology of S-CDs. The highly monodisperse spherical structure of S-CDs, as seen in Fig. 1
a, suggested that the sample demonstrated a high degree of dispersion stability and no tendency towards aggregation. Based on statistical analysis, the average size of S-CDs particles was 1.44 ± 0.01 nm (Fig. 1 b). The two-dimensional and three-dimensional images from the AFM testing both exhibited the highly stable dispersion of S-CDs, with an average height of approximately 1.6 nm ( Fig. 1 (c-e)), which aligned closely with the results obtained from HR-TEM analysis. Due to its small particle size and narrow size distribution, it increased reservoir porosity and permeability, improving reservoir physical properties and enhancing the fluidity of crude oil [42].

To address the low activity of CDs at the oil–water interface, we utilized the reaction between the –COOH on hydrophobic long chains (HCAB) and the –NH2 on the surface of CDs to induce an amide reaction, thereby grafting it onto the surface of CDs. The chemical properties of the modified nanoparticles were characterized using FT-IR and XPS (Fig. 2 ).

Fig. 2 a represented the FT-IR spectrum of S-CDs, where the peak at 3380 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds. The potent signals around 3275 cm−1 and 1551 cm−1 could be originated from N-H stretching vibration and bending vibration respectively, and the absorption peak at 2925 cm−1 could be associated with a potential C–H vibration mode. The stretching vibration of the CO bond produced a strong signal at 1665 cm−1, the absorption peak at 1391 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond, and the characteristic peak at 785 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of the C–C bond. These results showed that S-CDs possessed –OH, –NH2, and –CONH2 groups on their surface. XPS was employed to investigate the chemical state and composition of S-CDs. The results demonstrated the presence of C, N, and O elements, with atomic contents of 80.28 %, 10.92 %, and 8.28 %, respectively (Fig. 2 b). High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s was shown in Fig. 2 c, suggesting that O existed in S-CDs as CO. Similarly, high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 2 d) could be deconvoluted into N-C (399.8 eV), NH–(C)2 (401 eV), and N(C)4 + (402.4 eV), suggesting that HCAB was successfully grafted on the CDs surface. Based on C1s (Fig. 2 e), the deconvolution results validated the presence of CO (288.1 eV), CN/C-O (286.2 eV), and C–C (284.8 eV). The findings of the XPS analysis validated the findings of the FT-IR, indicating the presence of HCAB molecular chains and hydrophilic groups (–OH, –CONH2, and –NH2) on the surface of S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature and salt tolerance capabilities of S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature and salt tolerance of oil displacing agents was a prerequisite for improving the recovery rate of unconventional oil reservoirs. The particle size and zeta potential of S-CDs nanofluid before and after aging at different salinities were shown in the Fig. 3 . When the salinity of the SFW increased to 16 × 104 mg/L, the particle size of the hydration product was still less than 5 nm after aging for 7 d (Fig. 3 a), and the zeta potential was still less than −35 mV (Fig. 3 b), indicating that the S-CDs could still maintain high dispersion stability under the formation water salinity of 16 × 104 mg/L. When the concentration was higher than 16 × 104 mg/L, the particle size and zeta potential of the hydration products increased significantly, accompanied by the occurrence of agglomeration.

Fig. 4 illustrated the variations in S-CDs particle size and zeta potential before and after aging at various temperatures. Prior to aging, the particle size and zeta potential were 1.8 nm and −38.2 mV, respectively. Particle size and zeta potential remained relatively constant when the aging temperature was raised from 100 to 130 °C, demonstrating the exceptional stability of the S-CDs. The fact that S-CDs surface contained inert hydrophilic groups that helped to preserve stability to some degree was one of the main factors contributing to its resistance to high temperatures. When the temperature was higher than 130 °C, the particle size and zeta potential of the hydration products increased significantly, and the agglomeration phenomenon appeared obviously at 150 °C.

Achieving the wettability reversal of oil-wet rock surfaces and reducing the oil–water interfacial tension were crucial for increasing petroleum recovery. As shown in Fig. 5
a, there was a declining trend in the absorbance of S-CDs solutions (0.01–0.5 wt%) at different concentrations over time. Nonetheless, when the mass fraction was 0.05 wt%, the change range (K value) was the smallest, the lowest IFT at this concentration was 2.4 mN/m (Fig. 5 b). At lower concentrations (<0.05 wt%), S-CDs were continuously adsorbed onto the oil–water interface, making it impossible to achieve optimal interfacial tension. Nanoparticles aggregate in the liquid phase when the concentration exceeded 0.05 wt%, disrupting the orderly nanoparticle arrangement at the oil–water interface, leading to an increase in IFT. The existence of hydrophobic HCAB tail chains and hydrophilic surface groups (–OH, –CONH2, and –NH2) were significant contributors to the reduction of IFT. Therefore, S-CDs possessed high interfacial activity, diffusing to the oil–water interface to regulate its hydrophilic lipophilic balance.

Fig. 6
a illustrated the interfacial rheological properties of S-CDs. The nanofluid of S-CDs exhibited a remarkable interface modulus of 34.68 mN/m, which was significantly higher than that of the SFW (11.32 mN/m), H-CDs (12.45 mN/m) and Z-Si (22.65 mN/m), maintaining the high flow stability. Additionally, it displayed the lowest phase angle (Fig. 6 b). The S-CDs, with chemically active groups, could migrate towards the oil–water interface and create an adsorption layer. As the particle density at the interface gradually increased during the initial stage, the viscoelastic modulus of the interface experienced a rapid surge, thus altering the interfacial properties of the oil–water interface. S-CDs demonstrated interfacial activity and could be effectively absorbed into the interface layer, thereby enhancing the oil–water interface’s capability to recover from elastic deformation.

To reveal the surface wettability enhancement mechanism of S-CDs, hydrophobic probes (treated with 5 wt% N-dodecane thiol ethanol) in AFM testing were used to measure the adhesion of cores treated with different nanomaterials (H-CDs, Z-Si, S-CDs) to alkanes. To demonstrate the wetting capability of S-CDs in modifying the hydrophobic surface, the alteration of underwater oil contact angle over time was assessed. The oil contact angle was measured at 30-min intervals (the contact angle results at 0 h, 12 h, and 24 h were presented in the paper).

The hydrophobically treated AFM tip exhibited higher adhesion in the hydrophobic region, conversely displaying lower adhesion in the hydrophilic region. The insets in Fig. 7
(a-d) depicted adhesion maps of various core surfaces, where the white areas represented regions of higher adhesion force, while the black areas signified lower adhesion. The histogram in Fig. 7 depicted the frequency distribution of the adhesion force of the core surface after adsorption of different nanoparticles. The adhesion range of the rock surface treated with SFW ranged from 0 to 5 nN (center was 2.7 nN). Non-uniform distribution between white and black areas suggested uneven hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties on its surface (Fig. 7 a). Following the adsorption of H-CDs (Fig. 7 b), Z-Si (Fig. 7 c), and S-CDs (Fig. 7 d), the adhesion was reduced and the distribution became narrower. Particularly after the adsorption of S-CDs, the adhesion force range was from 0 to 500 pN, with the peak concentrated at 10 pN. This change was attributed to the alteration in wetting properties on the core surface after the adsorption of S-CDs, from uneven to uniform distribution, resulting in a significant decrease in adhesion force on the core surface. This indicated that S-CDs had a smaller particle size and a large number of hydrophilic functional group structures on the surface compared to H-CDs and Z-Si, forming a more uniform covering layer on the rock surface and exhibiting the lowest adhesion force [43]. The lower adhesion force made it easier for oil droplets to detach from the core, thereby improving oil recovery efficiency.

The glass slide treated with lipophilic treatment in SFW maintained an underwater oil contact angle of 31° nearly unchanged within 24 h ( Fig. 7 e). This was due to SFW lacking wetting control properties, retaining the surface in an oil-wet state. Under the influence of 0.05 wt% H-CDs nanofluid, the oil contact angle increased to 60°, indicating the presence of certain wetting control capabilities in H-CDs. When exposed to 0.05 wt% Z-Si nanofluid, the initial oil contact angle was 104°, showcasing its strong wetting control ability by transitioning the glass slide surface from oil-wet to water-wet. However, after 24 h of static exposure, the contact angle decreased to 71°, suggesting the instability of Z-Si hydrophilic structures. However, within 0.05 wt% S-CDs nanofluid, the initial underwater oil contact angle was 105°, increasing to 139° within 24 h. The above results indicated that even though the surface of S-CDs were modified by grafting long-chain alkanes, they still maintained strong hydrophilicity. This property enabled them to reduce the oil affinity on the glass surface, thereby achieving wetting reversal. The relatively stable hydrophilic structure endowed S-CDs with the ability to continuously enhance their wetting properties.

As shown in Fig. 8
a, it demonstrated the emulsification performance and emulsion stability experiments of different nanofluids (Voil: V nanofluid = 1:5, 10 × 1000 rpm for 10 min). S-CDs nanofluids had a significantly better emulsifying ability than other nanofluids, and there was no obvious demulsification phenomenon within 10 min. The O/W emulsion with strong stability was formed and continued to migrate, effectively improving the fluidity of the mixture. Water separation rate was also an important evaluation of emulsification-demulsification performance [44]. After 120 min, the water separation rate reached 100 %, indicating that the emulsion could spontaneously and quickly break the emulsion to prevent formation plugging [45]. It was observed under an optical microscope that the emulsion state of the S-CDs mixed liquid was stable after standing for 120 min ( Fig.S1 ), which was consistent with the analysis results of water separation rate.

To reveal the role of hydrophobic long chains grafted on the surface of S-CDs during oil film peeling, hydrophobic probes (treated with 5 wt% N-dodecanethiol) were used in AFM testing to measure their adhesion to alkanes.

The oil-washing efficiency of different nanometer particles was directly assessed through oil film detachment experiments. To simulate reservoir conditions, the crude oil was placed on a glass surface and aged for 7 d at 130 °C. The nanofluid concentrations were all at 0.05 wt%. As depicted in Fig. 9
(a, b), SFW exhibited a relatively low oil film detachment efficiency, with the remaining oil film area accounting for 43.6 %. Glass plates with adhered oil films were immersed separately in H-CDs and Z-Si nanofluids for 24 h, resulting in oil film detachment ratios of 94.7 % and 95.7 %, respectively. However, after 24 h of immersion in S-CDs nanofluid, the remaining oil area ratio was only 1.1 %, and the oil film was almost peeled off. The detachment of the oil film mainly occurred at the oil-solid interface ( Fig. 9 c). S-CDs, due to their smaller particle size and stronger hydrophilic properties, reduced interactions between the oil film and oleophilic surface. At the same time, the hydrophobic long chains on the surface of S-CDs had a strong adhesion force (10–30 nN) with crude oil ( Fig. 9 d), causing the hydrophobic long chains to deeply embed into the crude oil, exposing the hydrophilic groups on the surface of S-CDs to contact the surface of crude oil. Therefore, S-CDs could firmly adsorb on the surface of crude oil, causing the original interface of the crude oil to loosen. Under the “push-pull” action of S-CDs at both the oil-solid interface and the oil–water interface, the oil contact angle gradually increased, causing the oil film to contract until it eventually detached [46]. Therefore, effectively controlling the wetting properties at the oil-solid interface could significantly enhance the efficiency of residual oil recovery.

The detachment efficiency of oil films was directly related to the adsorption of nanoparticles at the solid–liquid interface. Additionally, the adsorption characteristics of nanoparticles at the oil–water interface also had a significant impact on the efficiency of oil film detachment. Therefore, to explore the influence of nanoparticles adsorbed at the oil–water interface on oil film detachment, a visualization of the microscopic pore-throat model simulating reservoir flow channels was employed, dodecane was used to simulate residual oil films, while a Micro-Particle Image Velocimetry (μ-PIV) was utilized to visually measure the flow field of the fluid surrounding the oil phase [47,48].

According to the Fig. 10
(a-d), the left side showed the channel wall, and the right side showed the flow velocity distribution field of the oil–water interface. The flow velocity of all four systems decreased from left to right, and the highest flow velocity near the left channel wall was about 0.0042 m/s (Fig. 10 e), but there were significant differences in the flow velocity of the four systems at the oil–water interface. In SFW, the flow rate on the surface of the oil droplets was about 0.0022 m/s, while in S-CDs nanofluids, the flow rate at the oil–water interface was the slowest (0.00091 m/s). This was because S-CDs had strong oil–water interfacial activity (affinity of hydrophobic long chains to crude oil) and smaller particle sizes, making them easily adsorbable at the oil–water interface, formed a nanoscale particle film, and when the fluid flushed the oil drop surface, the oil drop surface solidified and the surrounding flow field changed [49]. As a result, there was an increase in shear force, making it easier for the oil drop to rupture when flushed by the fluid. The ruptured small oil drops were not easily re-adsorbed onto the solid surface due to the layer of particle film adsorbed on their surface, and they were also not easily merged with other oil drops. Therefore, they maintained a smaller size and were carried away with lower flow resistance [50].

The performance of prepared nanomaterials in tight oil development was assessed through core flooding experiments using low-permeability saturated cores with a permeability of 0.51 × 10−3 μm2. SFW, H-CD, Z-Si, and S-CDs nanofluid (0.05 wt%) were injected into the cores at a low rate of 0.1 mL/min, the trend of injection pressure changing with injection volume was depicted in the Fig. 11
a. The injection pressure of SFW sharply increased to a maximum value of 4.1 MPa, and then rapidly decreased. Throughout this process, the oil in the core was continuously displaced. Due to the capillary breakthrough force, the injection pressure stabilized at 3.3 MPa when 8.9 PV of SFW was injected. There were no oil droplets at the outlet, and the final oil recovery rate was 35.0 %. Similarly, the injection pressure trends of H-CD, Z-Si, and S-CDs nanofluids showed similar patterns, with a gentle initial slope followed by a gradual decrease. The injection pressures of H-CD nanofluid, Z-Si nanofluid, and S-CDs nanofluid stabilized at 3.4 MPa, 3.1 MPa, and 2.4 MPa, respectively, with oil recovery rates of 50.7 %, 53.6 %, and 69.9 % (Fig. 11 b). It was noteworthy that compared to SFW, H-CDs, and Z-Si, the S-CDs nanofluid exhibited the best oil displacement efficiency without adding any surfactants, with a pressure drop reduction rate of 32.4 % and an enhanced oil recovery of 34.9 %. To simulate the actual application scenarios in oil fields, S-CDs nanofluid flooding was performed after a primary water drive, followed by a secondary water drive. As shown in Fig.S2 , the enhanced oil recovery rate by secondary water flooding was 19.15 %, and the injection pressure was reduced by 31.87 %.

Based on this, the low interfacial tension and high interfacial activity of 0.05 wt% S-CDs nanofluid enabled easy adsorption at the oil–water and solid–liquid interfaces. As shown in Fig. 12 , S-CDs significantly reduced the adhesion between the rock surface and crude oil. Simultaneously, in the three-phase zone (oil/S-CDs nanofluid/hydrophilic surface), S-CDs formed a self-assembled wedge-shaped two-dimensional structure, generating disjoining pressure perpendicular to the oil–water interface, facilitating the easy detachment of oil droplets from the rock. The hydrophobic tail of S-CDs bound to the hydrophobic components on the rock surface, exposing hydrophilic head groups, thereby achieving wetting reversal. Besides, According to LCSM, after injecting S-CDs nanofluid, the wall roughness (Sa) changed from 7.633 μm dropped to 4.986 μm (Fig. 12 (b, c)), indicating that S-CDs were adsorbed into the rock, and the core surface was smoother at the macroscopic scale due to the filling of nanoparticles. Therefore, S-CDs could effectively improve the backward migration ability of crude oil emulsion. Table 2 summarized some previous work on enhanced oil recovery using nanofluids. Due to their strong temperature and salt resistance and high interfacial activity, S-CDs exhibited significant potential for increasing oil recovery and reducing pressure.

In summary, we synthesized small-sized (1.44 ± 0.01 nm) S-CDs using a simple two-step hydrothermal method. The introduction of long-chain alkanes onto the surface of S-CDs enhanced their interfacial activity while maintaining their hydrophilicity. As a result, S-CDs could effectively adsorb onto rock surfaces, achieved wetting reversal (oil-wet to water-wet), reduced the roughness of the core surface, lowered adhesion forces to approximately 10 pN, and generated sufficient separation pressure to detach oil from the rock surface. S-CDs were adsorbed at the oil–water interface, the formed oil droplets were more likely to break during flushing, forming a stable emulsion and facilitating continued migration. Remarkably, even without any surfactants, 0.05 wt% S-CDs nanofluid demonstrated excellent pressure reduction capabilities (32.4 %) and increase the recovery rate by 34.9 % (compared to SFW). This study provides a feasible method for synthesizing multifunctional carbon nanoparticles without surfactants, which can be utilized for enhanced oil recovery and injection pressure reduction in tight reservoirs.

Wei Xiang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yue Sun: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lin Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Xiaolong Wang: Investigation. Mingwei Zhao: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Caili Dai: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (NO. 2022YFE0203400), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52174054), Climb Taishan Scholar Program in Shandong Province (tspd20230605), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (23CX07010A).
